news_summary: Advancements in light-powered chips promise to boost AI and quantum computing, while discoveries of metabolically active microbes in Ötzi's frozen remains offer new insights into ancient life.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans le domaine des puces alimentées par la lumière devraient donner un nouvel élan à l'intelligence artificielle et à l'informatique quantique, tandis que la découverte de microbes métaboliquement actifs dans les restes gelés d'Ötzi apporte un nouvel éclairage sur la vie dans l'Antiquité.
news_summary_es: Los avances en los chips alimentados por luz prometen impulsar la inteligencia artificial y la computación cuántica, mientras que los descubrimientos de microbios metabólicamente activos en los restos congelados de Ötzi ofrecen nuevos conocimientos sobre la vida en la antigüedad.

articles.title: New light-powered chip could accelerate AI and quantum computing
articles.summary: Scientists have created a tiny chip that can generate, steer, and read light-based information all in one device, marking a major leap toward ultra-fast, energy-efficient computing. The breakthrough uses atomically thin materials and nanoscale structures to control a unique quantum property of light called the “valley” degree of freedom, allowing information to be encoded in new ways.
articles.content:
Scientists at Monash University have created a tiny new circuit that can generate, direct, and read information carried by light, all within a single chip.

The advance marks a significant milestone for a growing area of research known as "valleytronics," which could help drive future breakthroughs in faster computing, lower energy consumption, and quantum technologies.

Developed by researchers from the Monash School of Physics and Astronomy, the new device combines advanced nanotechnology with cutting-edge materials to solve a challenge that has limited the field for years.

For the first time, the team has built a fully integrated chip capable of producing specialized light signals, steering them along specific paths, and converting them into electrical signals within the same compact system.

These signals store information using a quantum property called the "valley degree of freedom." Scientists believe this unique characteristic could provide entirely new ways to encode, transmit, and process data.

Integrated Valleytronics Chip Solves Long-Standing Challenge

Lead author Dr. Chi Li, whose team's findings were published in Nature Photonics, said the achievement addresses a major obstacle in valleytronics research.

"Until now, we could generate or detect these signals, but not do everything in one integrated device," Dr. Li said.

"What we've built is a complete on-chip system that can create, route and read this information with very high precision."

The device relies on ultra-thin materials that are only a few atoms thick. These materials are paired with specially engineered nanostructures designed to precisely control light at extremely small scales.

Dr. Kaijian Xing, co-first author of the study and a Research Fellow at Monash University, explained that the team developed a practical way to combine these components.

"We employ a straightforward stacking approach to integrate ultra-thin materials with metasurfaces, overcoming the technical challenges of direct material growth on photonic structures, and enabling further advances in valleytronics," Dr. Xing said.

Room-Temperature Photonic Technology

One of the technology's most important advantages is that it operates at room temperature. Many quantum systems require extremely cold environments, making them more difficult and expensive to use in real-world applications.

Senior author Dr. Haoran Ren, ARC Future Fellow and leader of the Monash NanoMeta Group, said the work could pave the way for a new generation of compact photonic devices that are both programmable and highly efficient.

According to Dr. Ren, the technology could support faster computing systems, reduce energy consumption, and enable new methods for secure communications and advanced data processing.

"This is a significant step toward scalable, chip-based technologies that use light instead of electricity to process information," Dr. Ren said.

"Photonic devices use light to achieve massive bandwidths, ultra-fast data transmission speeds, and lower energy consumption, so what we have achieved has strong potential for applications in quantum computing, advanced imaging, and next-generation optical communication systems."

Processing Multiple Streams of Information

To demonstrate the chip's capabilities, the researchers successfully encoded and processed two separate images at the same time. The experiment showed that the device can manage multiple streams of information simultaneously, an important feature for future computing technologies.

Professor Stefan A. Maier, Head of the School of Physics and Astronomy and Nanophotonics Laboratory at Monash University, said the development helps bridge the divide between fundamental scientific discoveries and practical technologies.

"This is an important step toward fully integrated valleytronic systems," said Professor Maier. "By combining light and quantum materials on a chip, we can access new ways of encoding and processing information."

The international project brought together researchers from Australia, China, Singapore, Germany, and Japan, combining expertise in nanophotonics, two-dimensional materials, and optoelectronics.

The Monash University team included Dr. Chi Li, Dr. Kaijian Xing, Professor Michael S. Fuhrer, Professor Stefan A. Maier, and Dr. Haoran Ren. Additional contributions came from the Singapore University of Technology and Design, LMU Munich, and the University of Technology Sydney.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in integrated valleytronics technology, enabling a chip that can generate, steer, and read light-based quantum information at room temperature. This advance promises faster, energy-efficient computing and new quantum applications with broad societal impact. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a major step toward practical photonic and quantum computing technologies.
articles.personality_title: New light-powered chip advances ultra-fast, energy-saving computing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been working on ways to use light instead of electricity to process information. This approach, called valleytronics, uses a special property of light to encode data. Until now, devices could only do parts of this process, limiting progress.

**What happened** – Researchers at Monash University created a tiny chip that can generate, steer, and read light signals all in one device. These signals carry information using the “valley degree of freedom,” a quantum feature of light. The chip uses materials just a few atoms thick combined with tiny structures to control light precisely. It works at room temperature, which is easier and cheaper than other quantum devices that need very cold conditions.

**Impact** – This is the first fully integrated valleytronics chip, allowing new ways to encode and process information faster and with less energy. The device also showed it can handle two images at once, proving it can manage multiple data streams. This could lead to smaller, more powerful computers and better communication technologies.

**What's next step** – The team plans to further develop this technology to create practical, programmable photonic devices. These could be used in quantum computing, secure communication, and advanced imaging. The research also opens doors for more collaborations worldwide to improve and apply valleytronics.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists built a tiny chip that uses light to process information faster and more efficiently, marking a key step toward new computing technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle puce fonctionnant à la lumière ouvre la voie à un calcul ultra-rapide et économe en énergie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à utiliser la lumière plutôt que l’électricité pour traiter l’information. Cette méthode, appelée valléetronique, utilise une propriété spéciale de la lumière pour coder les données. Jusqu’à présent, les appareils ne pouvaient effectuer qu’une partie du processus, freinant les progrès.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Monash ont créé une petite puce capable de générer, diriger et lire des signaux lumineux en un seul appareil. Ces signaux transportent des informations grâce au « degré de liberté de vallée », une caractéristique quantique de la lumière. La puce utilise des matériaux d’une épaisseur de quelques atomes combinés à de minuscules structures pour contrôler la lumière avec précision. Elle fonctionne à température ambiante, ce qui est plus simple et moins coûteux que d’autres appareils quantiques nécessitant des températures très basses.

**Impact** – C’est la première puce valléetronique entièrement intégrée, permettant de nouvelles façons de coder et traiter l’information plus rapidement et avec moins d’énergie. L’appareil a aussi montré qu’il peut gérer deux images simultanément, prouvant qu’il peut traiter plusieurs flux de données. Cela pourrait conduire à des ordinateurs plus petits et plus puissants ainsi qu’à de meilleures technologies de communication.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit de développer davantage cette technologie pour créer des dispositifs photoniques programmables et pratiques. Ils pourraient être utilisés en informatique quantique, communication sécurisée et imagerie avancée. Cette recherche ouvre aussi la voie à plus de collaborations internationales pour améliorer et appliquer la valléetronique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont construit une petite puce qui utilise la lumière pour traiter l’information plus vite et plus efficacement, marquant une étape clé vers de nouvelles technologies informatiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo chip alimentado por luz impulsa la computación ultra rápida y eficiente
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estado buscando usar la luz en lugar de electricidad para procesar información. Este método, llamado valletrónica, usa una propiedad especial de la luz para codificar datos. Hasta ahora, los dispositivos solo podían hacer partes del proceso, limitando el avance.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Monash crearon un chip pequeño que puede generar, dirigir y leer señales de luz en un solo dispositivo. Estas señales transportan información usando el “grado de libertad de valle”, una característica cuántica de la luz. El chip usa materiales de solo unos pocos átomos de grosor junto con estructuras diminutas para controlar la luz con precisión. Funciona a temperatura ambiente, lo que es más fácil y barato que otros dispositivos cuánticos que necesitan temperaturas muy bajas.

**Impacto** – Es el primer chip de valletrónica totalmente integrado, permitiendo nuevas formas de codificar y procesar información más rápido y con menos energía. El dispositivo también mostró que puede manejar dos imágenes a la vez, demostrando que puede procesar múltiples flujos de datos. Esto podría llevar a computadoras más pequeñas y potentes y mejores tecnologías de comunicación.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea seguir desarrollando esta tecnología para crear dispositivos fotónicos programables y prácticos. Podrían usarse en computación cuántica, comunicaciones seguras e imágenes avanzadas. La investigación también abre puertas para más colaboraciones internacionales para mejorar y aplicar la valletrónica.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos construyeron un chip pequeño que usa luz para procesar información más rápido y eficientemente, marcando un paso clave hacia nuevas tecnologías de computación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a delicate, ultra-thin layered chip glowing softly as streams of colorful light weave precisely through intricate nanostructures on its surface, symbolizing the seamless integration of light-based signals and quantum properties within a compact, futuristic circuit.

articles.title: Ötzi's frozen remains may harbour metabolically active microbes
articles.summary: Researchers studying a 5300-year-old mummified man have identified bacteria that lived in his gut when he was alive, as well as cold-tolerant fungi that colonised his body after death
articles.content:
Researchers studying a 5300-year-old mummified man have identified bacteria that lived in his gut when he was alive, as well as cold-tolerant fungi that colonised his body after death

The mummy is preserved in a refrigeration chamber at a constant temperature of -6°C South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology/Eurac Research/Marion Lafogler

Some of the microbes lingering on the 5300-year-old remains of “Ötzi the Iceman” may still be metabolically active, despite being kept in icy conservation conditions.

Ötzi’s mummified body was discovered in 1991 thawing out of an Alpine glacier close to the border of Austria and Italy. He is estimated to have lived at some point between 3350 and 3120 BC, and in the 35 years since he was found, studies of his remains have revealed a treasure trove of information, including that he was probably dark-skinned and balding, had numerous tattoos and had a wound in his shoulder from an arrow, suggesting he was murdered.

Ötzi is now kept at the South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology in Bolzano, Italy, in conditions designed to mimic some of those inside the glacier where he was found: a temperature of -6°C (21°F) and a relative humidity of 99 per cent.

Frank Maixner at Eurac Research’s Institute for Mummy Studies in Bolzano and his colleagues have analysed the bacteria and fungi found in skin swabs, tissue fragments and internal thawed water samples from the mummified remains taken in 1992, 2010 and 2019 and compared them with soil and ice samples collected from the discovery site in the 1990s.

On Ötzi, they found both ancient and modern-day microbes, some of which may be metabolically active. “We can really distinguish between the Iceman’s endogenous gut bacteria and microbes that joined from the environment as soon as he died,” says Maixner.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Each month, Michael Marshall unearths the latest news and ideas about ancient humans, evolution, archaeology and more. Sign up to newsletter

The team’s metagenomic analysis of internal tissues revealed specialist bacteria that thrive without oxygen inside the mammalian gut, including species of Treponema and Kineothrix. Based on the level of damage to the DNA of these bacteria, which accumulates over time, the bacteria probably lived inside Ötzi when he was alive.

There was a wide variety of microbes present , as seen in other prehistoric gut microbial communities, which may reflect the more varied diet of Copper Age humans compared with modern Western societies, says Maixner.

They also found Pseudomonas bacteria – commonly found in soil and water – in all samples. The DNA damage of these bacteria indicates that they probably belong to an ancient community from the discovery site, says Maixner.

On the external Ötzi samples, the team uncovered cold-loving, or psychrophilic, yeasts, including Phenoliferia, Glaciozyma, Goffeauzyma and Mrakia.

DNA damage indicated that these were also ancient microbes, but the abundance of Glaciozyma increased between 2010 and 2019, and it became the dominant strain, while the level of DNA damage dropped. This suggests it may be metabolically active, or able to replicate under the conservation conditions.

Reconstruction of Ötzi South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology/Augustin Ochsenreiter

“I think it’s good evidence that Glaciozyma colonised the mummy post mortem. They seem to be growing for some reason,” says Nikolay Oskolkov at Lund University in Sweden, but he would like to see more data points to show that the results aren’t a result of experimental procedure.

The growing abundance of this yeast is potentially very interesting, says Damla Kaptan at the University of Stavanger in Norway. But we can’t be sure it is active until we also look for RNA that would be produced from the yeast DNA, which would indicate that its genes are turned on. “There is still the possibility that the yeast remained dormant or became active to some extent during thawing,” she says.

Some of these yeasts encode enzymes for breaking down protein and collagen, so they could harm the mummy, but the team saw no evidence of damage.

The team also found that some of the microbes contain genes required for degrading the toxic compound phenol. Maixner suggests this may be a side effect of researchers in the 1990s treating the mummy to kill off fungi. “When the Iceman was found, there was already active mould formation and they treated him with a phenol-containing substance,” he says. “Perhaps the microbiome was enriched by the treatment.”

Maixner says the study shows that Ötzi isn’t a biologically frozen time capsule, but a complex ecosystem shaped by the succession of his gut microbes after he died, the infiltration of organisms from the glacier over thousands of years and then three decades of conservation. “As these microorganisms have been on the mummy since the beginning, should they be considered as part of the remains?” he says.

He recommends regular genomic surveillance, including looking for other signs of activity like RNA and metabolites produced by microbes, to check if the microbial communities have really awoken from dormancy and are degrading the mummy’s tissues. If so, scientists might need to consider whether conservation conditions should be changed, perhaps by lowering the temperature or humidity to further restrict microbial activity.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about metabolically active microbes in a 5300-year-old mummy, offering new insights into ancient microbiomes and conservation science. This breakthrough has broad implications for archaeology, microbiology, and preservation techniques, benefiting scientific understanding and heritage conservation.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find living microbes in 5,300-year-old mummy Ötzi
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Ötzi the Iceman is a well-preserved mummy discovered in 1991 in the Alps, dating back over 5,300 years. He is kept in a museum in Italy at very cold temperatures to protect his body from decay.

**What happened** – Researchers studied samples from Ötzi’s body collected over nearly 30 years. They found bacteria that lived inside his gut when he was alive, plus special cold-loving fungi that settled on his body after he died. Some of these fungi appear to still be alive and active, even though the mummy is kept frozen.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that Ötzi’s body is not just a frozen relic but a living ecosystem with microbes that survived thousands of years. It gives scientists new clues about ancient human gut bacteria and how microbes behave in cold, preserved environments. This knowledge could help improve how we protect important ancient remains.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study the microbes further by looking for signs they are truly active, like RNA production. They also want to see if changes to the museum’s cold storage might better stop microbes from damaging the mummy over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found that some microbes in the 5,300-year-old mummy Ötzi are still alive, offering new insights into ancient life and preservation methods.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des microbes vivants découverts dans la momie Ötzi vieille de 5 300 ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Ötzi l'homme des glaces est une momie bien conservée découverte en 1991 dans les Alpes, datant de plus de 5 300 ans. Il est conservé dans un musée en Italie à très basse température pour éviter sa dégradation.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié des échantillons du corps d'Ötzi, prélevés sur près de 30 ans. Ils ont trouvé des bactéries qui vivaient dans son intestin de son vivant, ainsi que des champignons adaptés au froid qui se sont installés sur son corps après sa mort. Certains de ces champignons semblent encore vivants et actifs, malgré la conservation glacée.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que le corps d'Ötzi n'est pas juste un vestige figé, mais un écosystème vivant avec des microbes ayant survécu des milliers d'années. Cela apporte de nouvelles informations sur les bactéries intestinales anciennes et le comportement des microbes dans des environnements froids. Ces connaissances pourraient aider à mieux protéger les restes anciens.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent étudier davantage ces microbes en cherchant des signes d'activité réelle, comme la production d'ARN. Ils souhaitent aussi savoir si modifier les conditions de conservation au musée pourrait mieux empêcher les microbes d'endommager la momie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des microbes vivants ont été découverts dans la momie Ötzi vieille de 5 300 ans, offrant de nouvelles connaissances sur la vie ancienne et les méthodes de conservation.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos encuentran microbios vivos en la momia Ötzi de 5.300 años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Ötzi el Hombre de Hielo es una momia bien conservada descubierta en 1991 en los Alpes, que tiene más de 5.300 años. Se guarda en un museo en Italia a temperaturas muy bajas para evitar que se descomponga.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron muestras del cuerpo de Ötzi tomadas durante casi 30 años. Encontraron bacterias que vivían en su intestino cuando estaba vivo, además de hongos que aman el frío y que colonizaron su cuerpo después de su muerte. Algunos de estos hongos parecen seguir vivos y activos, a pesar de que la momia está congelada.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que el cuerpo de Ötzi no es solo un relicto congelado, sino un ecosistema vivo con microbios que han sobrevivido miles de años. Da a los científicos nuevas pistas sobre las bacterias intestinales antiguas y cómo los microbios actúan en ambientes fríos. Este conocimiento puede ayudar a mejorar la protección de restos antiguos importantes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar más a fondo los microbios buscando señales de actividad real, como la producción de ARN. También quieren ver si cambiar las condiciones de conservación en el museo podría impedir mejor que los microbios dañen la momia con el tiempo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos descubrieron que algunos microbios en la momia Ötzi de 5.300 años siguen vivos, ofreciendo nuevas ideas sobre la vida antigua y métodos de conservación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an ancient, translucent glacier encasing a symbolic silhouette of a human gut filled with glowing, diverse microscopic microbes, alongside delicate, icy-blue fungi with subtle luminous growths spreading gently over the surface, all set within a softly lit, cool-toned refrigeration chamber that suggests preservation and quiet ongoing life.
